summary: Matt Rocklin proposes dask, a minimal task scheduling framework for out-of-core nd-array computations that uses simple Python dictionaries to represent computation graphs. The post explains how existing systems like Blaze handle simple chunked computations but fail for complex inter-block dependencies like matrix multiplication. Dask represents tasks as dictionary key-value pairs where values can be data or unevaluated function calls with dependencies on other keys. The deliberately 'low-tech' approach using plain dictionaries rather than formal class hierarchies is designed to minimize framework buy-in and maximize portability. A proof-of-concept demonstrates a 7GB out-of-core dot product completing in three minutes. The system is positioned as infrastructure for library writers rather than end users, with higher-level tools like Blaze providing the user interface.
key_points: Blaze's simple chunk-apply-concatenate model fails for complex computations with inter-block dependencies like matrix multiplication
Dask uses plain Python dictionaries to represent computation DAGs, avoiding framework lock-in and enabling easy translation to other systems
The 'top' function enables expressing tensor operations using index notation that naturally extends to blocked versions
Out-of-core execution achieved by combining numpy-style indexing on HDF5/bcolz with lazy task graph evaluation
The system targets library writers as infrastructure, not end users who would use higher-level tools like Blaze
money_quotes: Why don't we build Task and Data classes and construct a Python framework to represent these things formally? Because people have to learn and buy in to that framework and that's hard to sell. Dictionaries are easier to sell.
This system is not appropriate for users; it's unPythonic, low-level, and LISP-y. However I believe that something like this would be an appropriate standard for infrastructural libraries.
We haven't done work yet. We only do work when we finally call dask.get on the appropriate key for one of the blocks.
There is a decent chance that, if you've made it this far in this blogpost, you already understand the spec.
I was able to write a reference implementation in a couple dozen lines.
Three minutes for a 7GB dot product.
key_insight: Complex out-of-core array computations can be elegantly expressed using nothing more than Python dictionaries representing task dependency graphs, minimizing framework investment while maximizing interoperability.
